text.fullText: 2/17/2026 RE: 91 FR 2008 To those responsible for considering: We are the Environmental Law Society (ELS) of Vermont Law and Graduate School. We are a student organization whose purpose is to promote environmental awareness and provide a forum for student participation in projects and action concerning current environmental issues. ELS strives to affect global, national, statewide, and local issues by pursuing specific goals and cooperating with other environmental groups. ELS provides a forum for critique and support of the legal system, current laws and regulations, and actions undertaken by other groups which concern the environment. We are first-, second-, and third-year law students, LLM students, and Masters’ students who are deeply concerned about the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed “clarifications” and procedural updates to the Clean Water Act Section 401 certification process. I. Background The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is charged with protecting “human health and the environment.”1 The EPA is the Administrator of the Clean Water Act (CWA) and oversees the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES). 33 U.S.C. § 1251(d); 33 U.S.C. § 1342. A permit issued by the EPA pursuant to NPDES is different from, but relevant to, the State permit program. 33 U.S.C. § 1342(b). The permitting program discerns between permissible, anticipated, or informed introductions of “pollutants,” and those that are impermissible. Foremost, the overarching purpose of the CWA and accompanying permitting system is “to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters.” 33 U.S.C. § 1251(a). Section 401 of the CWA is unique; it grants authority to states and tribes (those identified as receiving “treatment in a similar manner as a State” (TAS)) to permit or deny proposed activities that result in a discharge into waters of the United States and require a CWA permit. Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (Jan. 15, 2026). The EPA may act as a certifying authority where there is no authorized state or tribe, but otherwise, Congress granted the states and tribes authorization over certification directly. 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). Under Section 401, a CWA permit may not be granted without 401 certification unless certification by a state or tribe is waived. Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2010 (Jan. 15, 2026). Certification indicates the proposed permit meets state or tribal 1 https://www.epa.gov/aboutepa/our-mission-and-what-we-do water quality standards and effluent guidelines, as well as other appropriate provisions of state or tribal law.2 401 certification thus enables a “robust State and Tribal role in [] Federal licensing and permitting . . .” Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2010 (Jan. 15, 2026). II. Proposed 2026 Changes Certification provisions were changed for the first time in 50 years during the first Trump Administration pursuant to an Executive Order (Promoting Energy Infrastructure and Economic Growth) in direct conflict with established law. Clean Water Act Section 401 Certification Rule, 85 Fed. Reg. 42210 (July 13, 2020); See generally PUD No. 1 of Jefferson County v. Was. Dep’t of Ecology, 511 U.S. 700 (1994). The 2020 rule limited the review of states and tribes generally from addressing a permitted activity “as a whole” in their certification review. Id. at 712. This was a stark contrast to over 50 years of operating as enacted. Exemplifying state dissatisfaction with the change, the 2020 rule was vacated by the U.S. District Court for Northern District of California. See In re Clean Water Act Rulemaking, 568 F. Supp. 3d 1013 (Ca. N.D., 2021). The 2020 rule was stayed by a U.S. Supreme Court emergency docket decision. Louisiana v. American Rivers, 569 U.S. 1 (2022). The last final rule on the matter, promulgated in 2023, reinstated the “activity as a whole” provision which enables state and tribe authority over the same certification decisions since the CWA’s enactment. Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule, 88 Fed. Reg. 66558 (September 27, 2023). The complicated history of section 401is befuddled with the EPA’s recent decision to revise the rule again. In the 2026 certification rule, the review of states and tribes will again be choked to the limiting standards seen in 2020. Essentially, this means “certifying authorities cannot consider water quality impacts to waters beyond the waters of the United States receiving the discharge(s) from [a] project.”3 In July 2025, the EPA requested input on regulatory uncertainty and implementation changes under the 2023 rule. States, tribes, industry members, and the public were invited to provide their input. The Summary Report for the tribal consultation was profound: almost unanimous interest to keep the 2023 rule.4 Specifically, the report noted, “[s]everal Tribes noted the activity-based scope of certification and the other related definitions (e.g., water quality requirements, applicable waters, etc.) provide 2 https://www.nawm.org/wetlands-law/cwa/clean-water-act-section-401-water-quality-certification.html 3 https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2026-01/cwa401_proposed-rule-economic- analysis_jan2026_508c.pdf at page 15 4 https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2026-01/cwa401_tribal-consultation-summary- jan2026_508c.pdf. The report adds: “In their input, the Tribes unanimously support the definitions and procedures promulgated under the 2023 Rule, asserting the 2023 Rule had successfully facilitated Tribal water quality protections without creating implementation challenges or regulatory uncertainty. The Tribes urged the EPA to retain the current regulations and uphold its trust responsibility to Tribal nations.” Id. Tribes the authority to holistically evaluate the range of possible impacts a federally licensed or permitted project may have on water quality.” Id. The Summary Report from the states’ consultation was identical: “State agencies and State associations expressed concern and cautioned against revising the scope of certification and associated definitions. A few State agencies and State associations asserted the EPA lacks evidence to support their claims of implementation challenges with the 2023 Rule, and expressed concern the EPA may return to inflexible, administratively burdensome processes that created implementation challenges.”5 Both states and tribes, entities authorized by Congress to certify CWA permits in their jurisdiction, relayed that the operational process under the 2023 rule is unproblematic, procedurally efficient, and balances sovereign interests well. Another significant change in the proposed 2026 rule includes limiting the conditions states and tribes may impose on a certification. Specifically, it narrows review to water quality concerns as opposed to the accepted general environmental protection concerns. The economic analysis announced alongside the proposed rule concluded that the number of conditional permit certifications decreases. The reduction arises because of increasing project developments, but the blatant fallacy is assuming that states and tribes will be more likely to certify projects when the Administrator has tightened its fist. In reality, reducing review will create a less trustworthy and transparent process by incentivizing sovereign entities to bolster their water quality standards and outright deny permits rather than suggest conditional permits. This presents an unfortunate outcome if the goal is to expedite development. III. The EPA is exceeding its statutory authority The EPA has exceeded its statutory authority under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by proposing a rule unsupported by substantial evidence. Agency actions are reviewed by courts under the APA, which “delineates the basic contours of judicial review.” Loper Bright Enters v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 391 (2024). The court reviews and sets aside agency actions that are found arbitrary or capricious; a denial of a statutory right; contrary to constitutional right; in excess of jurisdiction or authority; based on erroneous facts; unwarranted by substantial evidence; or otherwise contrary to law. 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A-F). Additionally, the Chevron doctrine guided statutory interpretation when reviewing statues administered by agencies. Loper Bright Enters v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 379 (2024). The Chevron doctrine required heavy deference to agency action by the reviewing court. Loper Bright Enters v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 379 (2024). Loper Bright, however, overruled Chevron and now requires courts to 5 https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2026-01/cwa401_federalism-consultation- summary_jan2026_508c.pdf exercise their “independent judgement” when deciding whether agency action adhered to its statutory authority. Loper Bright Enters v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 412 (2024). The EPA has exceeded its statutory authority under the APA by making decisions unsupported by substantial evidence. The agency sought tribal and state input which yielded disinterest for less involvement in the CWA permitting process. Local communities know best how to manage their ecosystems, and the EPA is interfering with local efforts. Conditional permit issuance is a necessary compromise when competing interests. The EPA is also failing to uphold its statutory responsibility to protect “human health and the environment.” https://www.epa.gov/aboutepa/our- mission-and-what-we-do. The primary focus of the EPA should not be overlooked when the current system is unproblematic and functioning. Any reviewing court should not defer to the EPA when deciding the legality of the proposed rule that is unsupported by substantial evidence and unpopular by the groups most affected by the change in procedure. With deep concern, VLGS Environmental Law Society | ELS@vermontlaw.edu
